name: resource-groups
context: Create, inspect, tag, and delete Azure resource groups safely with the Azure CLI
info: Resource groups are the default lifecycle and blast-radius boundary for most Azure workloads. Agents use this skill to create environment boundaries, apply ownership tags, list and filter groups across subscriptions, inspect a group before provisioning dependencies, and delete whole stacks when teardown is intended. The underlying execution surface is `az group`, especially `create`, `show`, `list`, `update`, and `delete`.
triggers: tag a resource group in azure
create a new rg in azure
body:
# Azure Resource Groups

Resource groups are the fastest safe boundary for most Azure automation. If an agent needs a place to create a web app, storage account, Key Vault, or AKS cluster, it usually starts here.

~~~bash
az group create --name rg-dojo --location eastus --tags owner=agent env=dev
az group show --name rg-dojo
az group list --query "[?location=='eastus']"
az group update --resource-group rg-dojo --set tags.Owner=platform tags.CostCenter=eng
az group delete --name rg-dojo --yes --no-wait
~~~

The most important judgment call is not how to create a group, but when deletion is acceptable. `az group delete` is recursive for most resources in the group. Agents should confirm subscription, group name, and ownership tags before running it.

Use resource-group tags to encode ownership, environment, cost center, and retention policy. Those tags make later discovery and cleanup easier across subscriptions.

Once the group exists, continue with [[azure/storage]], [[azure/key-vault]], [[azure/app-service]], [[azure/functions]], or [[azure/aks]].

sections.title: Create groups idempotently
sections.body: `az group create` is effectively idempotent for the same name and location. Use it early in a runbook to establish the environment boundary, and include tags on first creation so later discovery and cleanup can rely on them.

sections.title: List and inspect
sections.body: `az group list` supports JMESPath filtering through `--query`, while `az group show --name ...` returns the full group document. Use list when the agent is exploring unknown state, and show when it already has the exact group name and wants to inspect tags, provisioning state, or ID.

sections.title: Tagging and metadata
sections.body: `az group update --set tags.Key=value` lets you merge or update tags without recreating the group. Ownership and environment tags are the most important for autonomous agents because they turn cleanup, reporting, and safety checks into deterministic filters.

sections.title: Delete safely
sections.body: `az group delete --yes` recursively removes the group and most contained resources. Add `--no-wait` when the runbook can return immediately, but keep the delete synchronous when the next step depends on confirmed teardown. Double-check subscription and spelling before delete, especially in tenants with similarly named environments.

links.context: Confirm the correct subscription before creating or deleting groups
Provision storage resources inside the group
Create shared secret storage inside the same group
Deploy web apps into a prepared group
Create Function Apps after the group boundary exists
Create AKS clusters in a dedicated or workload-specific group
Inspect activity-log events tied to group creation or deletion

related.note: The active subscription must be correct before create or delete operations
Storage accounts are commonly the first dependency created after the group
Terraform often manages the same lifecycle declaratively once naming and boundaries are settled

scripts.name: Manage Resource Group
scripts.description: Create, list, inspect, retag, or delete an Azure resource group with `az group`

AZURE_RESOURCE_GROUP_ACTION.description: One of list, show, create, update-tags, or delete

AZURE_RESOURCE_GROUP_NAME.description: Resource group name for show, create, update-tags, or delete

AZURE_RESOURCE_GROUP_TAGS.description: Space-separated tag pairs such as "owner=agent env=dev"

AZURE_RESOURCE_GROUP_TAG_FILTER.description: Filter value for list, for example owner=agent

AZURE_DELETE_WAIT.description: Set to true to wait for `az group delete` completion

AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID.description: Subscription ID or name to set before running the command

subscription.description: Subscription ID or name to activate first

id.description: ARM resource ID of the group

depends.reason: The wrapper can switch subscription context but still requires a valid Azure login
The execution wrapper is a Bash script

author: dojo-community
